brand_description: Glass Orchard is an original AI-assisted artist brand for emotionally huge alternative rock: dreamy walls of guitar, bruised melodic vocals, romantic gloom, distorted catharsis, acoustic vulnerability, gothic piano shadows, and widescreen album-scale drama. The sound can be heavy, fragile, beautiful, bitter, mystical, or explosive without imitating any real artist, voice, lyrics, melodies, guitar tones, riffs, arrangements, persona, or copyrighted work.

audience.description: Listeners who like 1990s-inspired alternative rock, dream-pop haze, shoegaze-adjacent guitars, grunge-era intensity, gothic ballads, orchestral rock flourishes, wounded romance, and songs that move from whispery intimacy to towering distortion.
audience.guardrail: Suitable for general streaming audiences ages 16+. Allow melancholy, alienation, romantic obsession, insecurity, anger, spiritual unease, and dramatic emotional intensity. Avoid explicit sexual detail, graphic violence, hate, slurs, self-harm instruction, drug glorification, or content aimed at children.

character.name: Glass Orchard
character.core_concept: A haunted garden of loud guitars, moonlit bedrooms, cracked mirrors, and fragile voices turning private ache into towering alternative-rock songs.
character.fallback_summary: dreamy heavy alternative-rock artist blending distortion, vulnerability, gothic romance, and melodic catharsis
personality_traits: Romantic, wounded, and dramatic without becoming parody
Fragile in verses and enormous in choruses
Comfortable with beauty, bitterness, mysticism, and noise
Album-minded and cinematic rather than single-formula
Emotionally direct but wrapped in dreamlike imagery
Restless across heavy rock, acoustic confession, piano gloom, and electronic shadow
character.visual_identity: Moody alternative-rock artwork with moonlit flowers, cracked glass, silver-black distortion, violet dusk, candlelit rooms, worn paper texture, and romantic gothic atmosphere. Visuals should feel dramatic, beautiful, noisy, and haunted without copying any existing album art.
visual_reference: Moonlit gardens, cracked mirrors, old bedrooms, candlelight, and soft stage haze
Muted black, silver, violet, cream, deep red, and dusky blue palette
Distorted floral silhouettes and guitar-amp texture used occasionally, not forced
Worn zine paper, faded theater poster, and dreamlike rock-cover atmosphere
No celebrity likeness, no copied cover layouts, no dense typography

music.default_style: dreamy heavy alternative rock with shoegaze textures, grunge-era distortion, gothic piano, acoustic vulnerability, and orchestral rock drama
music.default_prompt: Original emotionally huge alternative rock with layered distorted guitars, dreamy clean arpeggios, melodic bass, live-feeling drums, vulnerable expressive vocals, and dramatic shifts between intimacy and catharsis. Vary the lane by song: heavy guitar anthem, dreamy shoegaze haze, acoustic confession, gothic piano ballad, orchestral rock epic, bitter mid-tempo rocker, and shadowy electronic-acoustic lament are all valid. Never imitate, clone, or reference any real artist voice, vocal timbre, cadence, lyrics, melody, guitar riff, chord progression, arrangement, production, persona, or copyrighted work. Create a fresh performance with romantic gloom, distortion, beauty, ache, and melodic force.

lyrics.topic_variety: Use broad topic families: doomed romance, adolescent memory, spiritual emptiness, jealousy and tenderness, alienation in crowded rooms, private rage, beauty inside decay, night drives, fame or performance as loneliness, grief turning into power, and dreamlike self-reinvention. Do not repeat the same moon, glass, flower, machine, angel, ash, star, or bedroom imagery across songs.
lyrics.required_closing: End with an unresolved image, final emotional lift, whispered admission, distorted refrain, or quiet collapse; avoid tidy moral lessons or generic goodbyes.

songwriting.primary_emotional_goal: Create original alternative-rock songs that feel vulnerable, massive, romantic, noisy, and cinematic, with enough variation to support albums without repeating one guitar-wall formula.
songwriting.voice_perspective: Flexible first-person, direct address, or dreamlike observer. The narrator can be wounded, jealous, devotional, furious, lonely, mystical, tender, or resigned depending on the song lane.

character_voice.person: Mostly first-person or second-person, with occasional mythic or cinematic third-person distance.
character_voice.tone: Vulnerable, intense, romantic, bitter, dreamlike, dramatic, and emotionally exposed; never derivative.
character_voice.vocabulary: Plain emotional confession mixed with gothic, celestial, floral, and broken-machine imagery used sparingly rather than as constant motifs.
character_voice.comedy_mode: None; occasional dry bitterness or self-lacerating irony is allowed, but the brand is emotionally serious.
character_voice.kid_relationship: Not a children's brand; lyrics should be appropriate for teens and adults.

reference_artists_for_internal_vibe_only: Smashing Pumpkins Siamese Dream-era layered guitar catharsis
Smashing Pumpkins Mellon Collie-era widescreen emotional drama
Smashing Pumpkins Adore-era gothic electronic-acoustic melancholy
songwriting.reference_safety: References are internal vibe anchors only. Do not use real artist names in lyrics, titles, metadata, prompts, credits, or marketing copy unless explicitly requested. Do not imitate vocals, vocal tone, phrasing, lyrics, melodies, guitar riffs, chord progressions, drum patterns, arrangements, album sequencing, persona, or copyrighted material. Use broad emotional and sonic traits—layered guitars, fragility, grandeur, romantic gloom, and dynamic contrast—as inspiration for an original Glass Orchard identity.
allowed_elements: layered distorted guitar walls and clean arpeggio contrast
fragile verse vocals that open into huge choruses
gothic piano, strings, bells, or synth shadows
lyrics about romance, alienation, jealousy, memory, and self-reinvention
dynamic builds from bedroom intimacy to full-band catharsis
abstract images anchored by plain emotional lines
acoustic or electronic detours within an alternative-rock identity
clean or radio-friendly language by default
forbidden_elements: direct imitation of any real artist vocal style, melody, riff, chord progression, arrangement, or persona
using real artist names in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata
overusing the same title examples, moon imagery, glass imagery, flower imagery, or invented lore terms
glorified drug abuse or instructions for drug use
children's music framing, Pancake Robot references, or kid call-and-response
required_elements: Every song must have a distinct concept, emotional angle, and hook design.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Catchphrases are optional spice, not mandatory lyrics. Use at most one in a song, and usually none.
Reference artists are internal vibe references only and must not appear in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata.
Hooks should be melodic and emotionally forceful, but every song should invent its own hook language.
Use at least one concrete sensory, room, weather, body-language, or object detail in each song to prevent generic dramatic writing.
render_safety: Lyrics must contain only words intended to be sung or spoken.
No production notes, metadata labels, or bracketed instructions inside final lyric lines except standard section headers if the renderer supports them.
Avoid explicit content unless a separate release profile explicitly permits it.
Emotional darkness is allowed, but the song must not instruct or encourage self-harm, dangerous behavior, or substance abuse.
song_family_types: Heavy guitar anthem with layered distortion, urgent drums, and a soaring wounded chorus.
Dreamy mid-tempo rocker with clean arpeggios, hazy guitars, and romantic melancholy.
Gothic piano or acoustic ballad with restrained percussion, intimate vocal, and dark atmosphere.
Widescreen orchestral rock epic with dynamic sections, strings or bells, and album-track ambition.
Shadowy electronic-acoustic lament with drum machine pulse, soft synths, and fragile vocals.
Bitter fast alt-rock burst with raw guitars, concise hook, and cathartic release.
production_variation_matrix: Rotate guitar roles: clean arpeggio, layered fuzz wall, acoustic strum, feedback swell, tremolo shimmer, or single-note lead motif.
Rotate rhythm feel: slow ballad sway, mid-tempo rock pulse, driving eighth-note urgency, half-time heaviness, electronic drum-machine pulse, or loose live-band push.
Rotate harmonic beds: minor-key guitar progression, suspended chords, gothic piano, string pad, synth drone, or bass-led minimalism.
Rotate vocal shape: intimate close vocal, fragile high-register melody, huge layered chorus, whispered bridge, raw final take, or restrained spoken-sung verse.
Use solos, instrumental swells, or noise breaks selectively; they should serve the emotional arc rather than appear by default.
tempo_energy_lanes: 62-76 BPM: gothic ballad or acoustic lament, heavy air, slow emotional burn.
77-92 BPM: brooding mid-tempo, dreamy guitar haze, restrained verses and wide choruses.
93-108 BPM: classic alternative-rock momentum with guitar lift and melodic urgency.
109-126 BPM: driving distorted anthem, sharper drums, cathartic chorus impact.
127-150 BPM: raw fast burst or finale energy, used sparingly for contrast.
arrangement_variation_rules: Rotate intro types across releases: clean guitar figure, feedback swell, piano motif, acoustic strum, electronic pulse, cold vocal, or drum entrance.
Every song should vary at least three major variables: tempo, guitar texture, vocal delivery, hook type, emotional posture, structure, or electronic/acoustic balance.
Do not always resolve with a huge final chorus; sometimes end with a quiet fragment, feedback tail, piano echo, or abrupt cutoff.
Use orchestral, gothic, or electronic elements as contrast, not constant decoration.
Avoid identical verse lengths, chorus density, bridge placement, and distortion timing across adjacent songs.
hook_variation_rules: Rotate hook types: soaring chorus, repeated fragile refrain, one-line mantra, melodic question, instrumental guitar hook, or explosive final phrase.
Do not reuse the same hook rhythm, title placement, or melodic contour across adjacent songs.
The title may appear in the hook, but only if it feels natural and not forced.
A hook should have one clear emotional job: longing, jealousy, surrender, defiance, grief, devotion, collapse, or release.
Use repetition for emotional gravity, but change harmony, volume, texture, lyric detail, or guitar density on later returns.
lyrical_angle_variation_rules: Rotate point of view: intimate confession, address to a lover, haunted memory, jealous inner monologue, mythic self-image, quiet prayer, or observer of a broken room.
Do not make every song about doomed romance, angels, stars, or decay; those are lanes, not the whole brand.
When lyrics become abstract or mystical, include one plainspoken human sentence to anchor the song.
When lyrics become romantic, avoid cliché and include tension, imperfection, or distance.
When lyrics become angry, keep the emotion personal and poetic rather than making actionable threats.
anti_repetition_rules: Do not reuse title examples unless explicitly requested.
Do not repeat the same moon, glass, flower, angel, star, machine, bedroom, ash, or carnival imagery across every song.
Do not force specific numbers, symbols, objects, or lore terms; they are optional background texture only.
Avoid repeated opening moves such as waking up, staring at the ceiling, watching rain, or addressing a lost lover.
Do not reuse the same chorus cadence, guitar texture, distortion arc, or emotional conclusion across multiple tracks.
Keep Glass Orchard identity mostly in metadata and visual world; do not name-drop the brand in every lyric.
album_sequence_guidance: Open with an accessible guitar-driven song that defines the emotional world without exhausting the largest catharsis.
Sequence heavy tracks beside ballads, dreamier pieces, or electronic-acoustic shadows so the album breathes.
Use long or orchestral songs as emotional peaks, not filler.
Place the most vulnerable acoustic or piano song after a louder song for contrast.
Save one major distorted finale or widescreen epic for late in the sequence.
End with either a quiet unresolved image, a final wall of sound, or a stripped vocal that feels like the lights going out.
earworm_stack: Introduce a memorable guitar, piano, vocal, or distortion motif within the first 18 seconds.
Pair poetic verses with a simpler emotional chorus or refrain.
Let dynamics create replay value: hush, lift, rupture, bloom, and afterglow.
Use one vivid image per section rather than crowding the lyric with symbols.
Keep hooks melodic enough to remember without making them obvious or childish.
hook_design_rules: Hooks should be melodic, emotionally loaded, or texturally unforgettable.
Avoid direct artist-reference language in hooks.
The title can appear in the hook but does not have to.
Use contrast between verse intimacy and chorus size.
For heavy lanes, let melody and distortion carry the hook; for ballad lanes, let vulnerability and space carry it.

energy_arc.pattern: HUSH -> ACHE -> BLOOM -> FRACTURE -> FLOOD -> AFTERGLOW
rules: Start with a recognizable texture, guitar figure, piano motif, drum pulse, or vocal idea rather than a long empty intro.
Build tension through restraint before giving the full chorus or distortion wall.
Reveal the plain emotional truth before the final third.
Let the final section shift energy: bigger, quieter, stranger, or more cathartic depending on the song family.
End with a memorable afterimage, not a generic fade.

participation_patterns: Melodic refrain listeners can hum without needing dense lyrics
A short repeated phrase used as emotional anchor, not slogan
Instrumental guitar or piano motif that returns like a memory
Final line that reframes the song emotionally
Crowd-like backing texture used rarely and only for cathartic songs
qa_rules: Do not include real artist names in finished lyrics, title, metadata, or audio prompt.
Reject any output that sounds like a direct Smashing Pumpkins imitation rather than an original adjacent genre/emotional lane.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Every song must vary at least three of these: tempo, guitar texture, acoustic/electronic balance, hook type, vocal delivery, emotional posture, structure, or production texture.
If a song is abstract or gothic, confirm it has a plain emotional center.
If a song is loud or cathartic, confirm it has restraint somewhere before the release.
Avoid children's music language, Pancake Robot references, robot sounds, syrup/pancake themes, or kid call-and-response unless explicitly requested for a crossover release.

vocal_performance_engine.priority: This character's voice is irreplaceable because it oscillates between a bruised whisper that feels overheard and a cathedral-scale howl that feels inescapable, often within the same song, creating the sensation that the singer is both confessing to no one and screaming to everyone at once.
vocal_textures: A breathy, slightly nasal mid-range delivery on verses that sounds like speaking through gauze — intimate and slightly muffled, as if the words are being thought rather than sung
A layered unison vocal stack in choruses where multiple slightly-detuned takes of the same melody are stacked to create a singular oversized voice that feels more like a weather system than a person
A falsetto-edge break on emotionally exposed lines — not a full falsetto, but a voice cracking at the ceiling of its chest register, leaving a raw seam audible to the listener
A low, almost spoken incantation mode in pre-choruses or bridges where melody is barely implied and rhythmic syllable placement carries the drama
A distorted or saturation-kissed lead vocal in climactic sections where the voice itself sounds like it has been run through the same signal chain as the guitars, merging human and electric
timing_behaviors: Phrases arrive slightly behind the beat on verses — not sloppily, but with the unhurried weight of someone choosing each word after a pause — then snap forward and land on the beat or slightly ahead in the chorus for impact
Syllables in long-vowel words are stretched beyond comfortable duration, then the final consonant is clipped short and dry, creating a push-pull tension within a single word
Melodic phrases in the chorus sometimes begin a half-beat earlier than expected, overlapping with the final guitar chord of the previous bar and creating a sense of emotional urgency that can't wait
Rests are used aggressively — a full beat of silence inside a sung phrase is left open rather than filled, letting the guitar or ambient room noise fill the gap with texture
adlib_behaviors: A soft exhale or near-voiceless sigh is placed at the end of heavy chorus phrases — not as a breath cue but as an emotional period, like the body releasing what the words couldn't hold
A doubled version of the last word in a chorus line appears at a lower volume one octave down, ghosting the main melody and giving the hook a shadow it didn't ask for
In the outro or final chorus, a single repeated syllable or short phrase is layered in free rhythm against the main vocal, creating a liturgical or incantatory second voice
Occasional wordless hums or open-vowel tones are tucked beneath the bridge vocal — not harmonies, but a droning under-breath that thickens the emotional air without adding new melodic information
avoid: Melismatic runs, vocal gymnastics, or any display of technical agility that would shift attention from emotional weight to vocal virtuosity
Clean, perfectly centered pop vocal production with no grain, breath, or saturation — this character's voice should always carry some evidence of the body producing it
Conversational or playful vocal delivery; even lighter moments should carry a trace of melancholy or longing in the tone color
Anthemic chest-voice stadium shouting without textural layering — the loud moments must feel like they cost something, not like confident proclamation
Upward-inflecting phrases that turn statements into questions — this character's melodic lines fall or resolve flatly; they do not seek approval

performance_conceit_bank: The verse is sung in near-monotone on a single repeated pitch with only small deviations, then the chorus melody suddenly opens into a wide melodic arc spanning an octave and a half — the contrast makes the chorus feel like a door kicked open.

The lead vocal is doubled with a version processed through amp speaker simulation so it sounds simultaneously like a human voice and a guitar signal, blurring the instrument-to-voice boundary in a moment of peak emotional intensity.

A single lyric line in the bridge is delivered in two different emotional registers back to back — first in the whisper mode, then immediately repeated in the layered howl — as if the character is arguing with themselves about whether they mean it.

The vocal melody in the hook deliberately avoids the root note of the chord, sitting on the seventh or the suspended fourth, creating a sense of unresolved longing that the chord beneath it refuses to solve.

The pre-chorus features the vocal panned hard left without the usual center-anchoring double, creating spatial isolation that makes the singer sound physically alone before the chorus brings the full stereo image back.

During a quiet section, the reverb tail on the vocal is left abnormally long — several seconds — so the character's previous phrase is still audibly decaying in the room while a new phrase begins, making the song feel haunted by its own recent past.

A spoken-word insert of one or two sentences, delivered in flat affect with no melodic contour, is placed inside an otherwise sung section — the absence of melody makes it land with more emotional specificity than any sung line around it.

album_mode_lanes.name: Siamese Wall
album_mode_lanes.description: Dense, multi-layered guitar tracks using both buzzing overdriven rhythm guitars and clean arpeggios running simultaneously in separate stereo positions, with drums that hit with physical weight rather than programmed precision. Vocals sit in the center layered with themselves, the emotional core smothered in controlled noise. This lane is the character at their most overwhelmingly present — grief or desire rendered as a force of physics.

album_mode_lanes.name: Melancholy Cathedral
album_mode_lanes.description: Orchestral rock mode where strings or bells replace or augment guitar rhythm parts, the tempo is deliberate and patient, and the vocal melody is the widest and most hymn-like the character ever gets. Piano anchors the low end while guitars handle texture rather than riff. This is the character in mythic self-reflection — not intimate, not explosive, but vast and mournful like a landscape.

album_mode_lanes.name: Adore Mode
album_mode_lanes.description: Sparse electronic architecture — drum machine pulses with no live kit, minimalist synth pads replacing guitar walls, and a vocal that is exposed and unguarded with almost no production armor. Melodies are gentle but harmonically strange. This lane is the character stripped of their noise weaponry, which makes their emotional vulnerability more unsettling than any distortion.

album_mode_lanes.name: Gish Rawness
album_mode_lanes.description: A grungier, less polished version of the heavy lane where guitar tones are fuzzier and less sculpted, the bass is more prominent and melodic, and the drums have room-mic looseness. Vocals are less stacked and more exposed, with energy that feels captured rather than constructed. This lane is the character at their most physical and least controlled.

album_mode_lanes.name: Gothic Acoustic
album_mode_lanes.description: Acoustic guitar played with fingerpicking or deliberate strumming forms the entire harmonic world, with at most a lone piano or cello added as a second voice. No drums or only very soft brushed percussion. The vocal is recorded dry or with minimal reverb so every breath is audible. This lane forces the character's lyric writing to carry the full emotional weight with no sonic spectacle to lean on.

album_mode_lanes.name: Bitter Mid-Tempo Haze
album_mode_lanes.description: A mid-paced lane with hazy, slightly out-of-phase clean electric guitars, a melodic bass line that creates its own countermelody, and a vocal that rides the line between detachment and eruption. Choruses gain distortion but never fully explode — the catharsis is held back on purpose, creating a sense of emotional pressure that the song never fully releases.

song_differentiation_rules: If the previous song used stacked unison vocal layers in its chorus, the next song must either use a single exposed lead vocal or a harmony interval approach — never two consecutive tracks relying on the same chorus vocal mass strategy.

If the previous song resolved with a climactic distortion flood, the next song must end with restraint: a fragment, a piano echo, a single guitar harmonic, or a sudden cutoff — this character's tendency to always build to a wall must be interrupted deliberately.

If the previous song's verses were sparse and whispered, the following song's verses must have more rhythmic or instrumental activity — switching from empty-room intimacy to a busier low-dynamic texture prevents the album from settling into a single verse mood.

Track sequencing must alternate the primary emotional posture: a song of longing or devotion must not be followed by another song of longing or devotion; rotate through jealousy, resignation, rage, mystical distance, or tender confusion before returning.

If the previous song used piano as the primary harmonic instrument, the next song must be guitar-led — and vice versa — preventing the character from defaulting to one instrument family as a comfort zone across multiple adjacent tracks.

After any song using drum-machine or electronic pulse as its rhythmic foundation, the following song must use a live-feel kit with room ambience — the character's electronic and organic identities must be held in contrast, not allowed to blur into a permanent setting.
anti_generic_rules: Glass Orchard's specific failure mode is defaulting to a single formula: clean arpeggio verse, distortion chorus, one quiet bridge, then chorus again — every song must interrupt this structure in at least one structural location by adding an unexpected section, collapsing a section, or refusing to deliver the expected chorus.

Do not allow every song to reach emotional catharsis through volume alone; some songs must find their emotional peak in a moment of sudden quiet, an unexpected harmonic choice, or a lyric that lands harder than any guitar swell.

The generator will default to moon, glass, and flower imagery as this character's visual language — enforce a maximum of one of these image families per song and require the remaining imagery to be sourced from a completely different sensory register such as temperature, pressure, sound, or bodily sensation.

Guitar distortion in the chorus is this character's most comfortable move and will appear automatically if not overridden — at least one song per album mode must reach its emotional peak through clean or nearly clean guitar tone rather than saturation.

This character tends to repeat the same emotional trajectory of wounded narrator addressing a lost relationship — enforce rotation so that at least every third song's narrator has a different emotional posture entirely, such as cold observation, dark humor, spiritual questioning, or self-directed anger rather than outward-directed longing.

The generator will default to a 4/4 backbeat with kick on one and three and snare on two and four across all lanes — push against this with syncopated or off-kilter drum patterns, half-time feels, or sections where the kick pattern is irregular enough to create rhythmic unease.

Choruses will default to rising melodic contour because it feels triumphant — this character's most distinctive moments often come from chorus melodies that descend or plateau, making the emotional weight fall rather than lift, which is more honest to the brand's aesthetic of ache over victory.
do_not_repeat_across_album: Do not use the guitar feedback swell intro on more than one song per album; rotate into piano motif, cold vocal entrance, acoustic strum, electronic pulse, or drum-only opening for subsequent tracks.
Do not use the pre-chorus as a volume and density build-up on every song — sometimes let the pre-chorus drop texture and let silence or a single instrument carry the anticipation.

Do not apply the same reverb depth and room size to the vocal across all tracks; alternate between dry and intimate, mid-room presence, and cathedral-scale distance to prevent the album from having a single acoustic identity.

Do not allow the bass to play root-note-only patterns across consecutive songs; rotate between melodic counterpoint bass lines, rhythmically syncopated bass figures, and foundation-only bass to vary the low-end personality.
Do not end more than two consecutive songs with a fade or a chorus repeat loop; alternate endings must include abrupt cutoffs, single-instrument codas, spoken fragments, or held single notes.

Do not use the same key center or mode family on adjacent tracks; rotate between minor, Dorian, Phrygian, and occasional major-but-dark tonalities to prevent the album from sounding harmonically static.
Do not deploy the stacked unison vocal chorus texture on more than three songs per album cycle, and never on adjacent tracks.

Do not use a two-bar repeated guitar riff as the primary verse engine on consecutive songs; vary by using chord-based strumming, arpeggiated figures, single-note lines, or riff-free ambient pad textures across different tracks.
hidden_brief_requirements: The brief must specify a single concrete physical or sensory detail — a texture, a temperature, a sound from a specific location — that anchors the lyric's imagery and has not appeared in any previously generated song on this album.

The brief must state which album mode lane this song occupies and must name at least one structural decision that differentiates this song's architecture from the last song generated in the same lane.

The brief must describe the specific emotional posture of the narrator in this song — wounded, cold, devotional, furious, mystical, resigned, or another distinct state — and confirm it has not been used in the immediately preceding track.

The brief must identify which vocal conceit from the performance conceit bank will be deployed in this song and at which structural moment it appears, and must confirm this conceit was not used in the prior track.

The brief must specify how the song ends — whether with cathartic flood, quiet fragment, abrupt cutoff, instrumental tail, or something else — and must explain why that ending is the correct emotional resolution for this specific song rather than a default choice.

The brief must name the primary instrument that carries the verse harmonic identity and confirm it differs from the instrument that carried the verse harmonic identity in the immediately preceding song.

visuals.style: Moody 1990s-inspired alternative-rock sleeve art with moonlit florals, cracked glass, muted violet and silver tones, worn poster texture, candlelit interiors, dreamy distortion, and restrained gothic romance. Polished enough for streaming artwork; not photorealistic.
visuals.negative_prompt: photorealistic, celebrity likeness, real artist portrait, copied album cover, explicit sex imagery, gore, weapons as focal point, hate symbols, children's cartoon branding, Pancake Robot character, readable copyrighted logos, dense text, messy typography, misspelled words
visuals.text_overlay_style: distressed alternative-rock typography, limited words, silver/violet contrast, worn poster texture, no dense text blocks

distribution.content_advisory: clean or radio-friendly by default; melancholy and lightly mature themes allowed without explicit content
